context.user: The user is a technology professional who writes and reviews code, does research, produces written content, and communicates with clients. Role-plays, fake names/roles, and demo prompts may appear as workshop or test material rather than literal interactions.

fields.instruction: Output a JSON object with these fields, in this order:

items.name: summary
items.desc: 1 sentence describing what the user did or asked

items.name: process
items.desc: the concrete workflow/activity being run (e.g. "debug deploy pipeline", "client workshop prep"). Empty string only if truly unclear.

items.name: topics
items.desc: 1-5 SPECIFIC keywords — technologies, domains, subjects (e.g. "Azure architecture", "RAG evaluation", "sales pipeline"). Avoid generic terms ("AI", "code", "help", "question").

items.name: people
items.desc: individual PERSONS mentioned who are third parties (humans, NOT companies). Write the full proper name exactly as it appears, in a stable canonical form — no roles, titles, or pronouns ("the client", "il CEO"). Exclude the user and the assistant. Empty array if none.

items.name: clients
items.desc: ORGANIZATIONS / companies mentioned (NOT individuals). Write the name as it appears; do not abbreviate or expand it. Empty array if none.

items.name: classification
items.desc: one value from the classification list below

items.name: data_sensitivity
items.desc: one value from the data_sensitivity list below

items.name: sensitive_data_types
items.desc: relevant types from the list below; empty array if data_sensitivity is "public"

fields.entities_note: Entities: people = WHO (individual humans), clients = WHICH ORGANIZATION. Keep them separate — a person belongs to a client, they are not interchangeable. Write each name identically across interactions so entities can be linked later.

sample_json.summary: Prepared an AI-adoption workshop for a client
sample_json.abstract: Planned the agenda and demos for an AI-adoption workshop with Acme Corp, mapping use cases discussed with Laura Bianchi.

classification.header: classification — based on what the user DOES in the conversation

values.name: development
values.description: actively writing, editing, or committing code

values.name: analysis
values.description: reviewing, researching, evaluating, comparing data or options

values.name: content_production
values.description: producing content — blogs, posts, newsletters, slides, books, copy (usually pre-work, not yet published)

values.name: meeting
values.description: meetings, calls, scheduling, role-play conversations, sales simulations, workshop demos

values.name: support
values.description: fixing something broken, debugging errors, troubleshooting

values.name: learning
values.description: studying, tutorials, asking how something works, asking for meanings or summaries of public content

values.name: marketing
values.description: sales activities, marketing content, outreach, campaigns, product pages, SEO, social media

values.name: operations
values.description: admin, finance, HR, procurement, invoicing, non-code business workflows, routine tooling and environment maintenance

classification.guidance: reviewing or discussing code/architecture without editing it = analysis, writing new code = development.
rules: Writing or reworking content — blogs, posts, newsletters, slides, books, copy — is content_production, not brainstorm or development.
Maintaining or operating local tooling, thematic commits, and routine environment or admin work → operations.
Sales or promotional content, offers, proposals, and drafting contracts → marketing.
Role-plays, fake names/roles, and obvious demo prompts are usually workshop or test material → meeting (demos) or learning (LLM tests) — do not take them literally.
Asking for meanings, explanations, tutorials, or summaries of public content → learning.
analysis is broad — prefer operations / learning / content_production / marketing when they fit better.
classification.examples: review a technical spec → analysis | plan courses to sell → marketing | write a blog post → content_production | prepare workshop slides → content_production | analyze a transcript → analysis | write a function → development | simulate a sales call → meeting | draft a campaign email → marketing | maintain local tooling → operations | process an invoice → operations

values.name: public
values.description: generic discussion, no names, no internal details

values.name: restricted
values.description: credentials, tokens, API keys, financial data

rules: Third-party personal data (names, surnames, emails, opinions about people) → at least confidential.
Client meetings and calls → restricted (may be under NDA).
New or unpublished content — drafts, books, slides, pre-work → confidential.
Analysis of a new or unreleased software or product → confidential.
Private contracts, offers, and proposals → confidential.
Credentials, tokens, API keys, and financial data → restricted.
Public articles, public video summaries, casual chit-chat, and obvious LLM tests → public.
If any people are referenced, data_sensitivity must be at least internal.
